paragraphs: You have a Greek toe form with very narrow forefoot and a very wide heel. A mixed profile; forefoot and heel require different fits.

Beyond the obvious: Given your Greek toes, rather avoid very pointy shoes. These might squeeze your toe tips, even if the width of the shoe fits you. Your shallow heel doesn't project as far back as most. Deeply sculpted cups will feel empty at the back.
interpretation.title: Your Foot Shape

paragraphs: Your Scarpa Furia Air (EU 38.5, 0.5 down) is rather relaxed for Scarpa, where the usual downsize is 1.5 sizes.
Your Scarpa Furia Air's empty heel is most likely a sizing problem, you could try a size smaller for a better fit.
interpretation.title: What Your Current Shoe Fit Tells Us

paragraphs: Based on your scan and current shoe fit, we target shoes with Greek toe form, narrow forefoot width, and wide heel width.
Given your preference for sport climbing outdoors on limestone and a balanced fit, we prioritize balanced-stiffness velcros or lace-ups with slight downturn and slight asymmetry.
Note: data on shoe heel depth is unfortunately not widely available. We're working on extracting this data from shoe scans, but for now the recommendations match heel width only.
The softer, stiffer, and budget tiers below offer alternative options around this target.
interpretation.title: What to Look For

recommendations.description: Lace-up with Greek toe form, medium forefoot and a wide heel cup. 4.5mm Red Chili RX-2 Techgrip rubber with a full, medium-hard midsole, resulting in a medium-firm shoe with slight downturn and slight asymmetry.
recommendations.tradeoffs: Tradeoff: Slightly wider in the forefoot and slightly stiffer than we target.

recommendations.description: Velcro with Greek toe form, medium fit throughout. 4mm CAT 1.5 rubber with a full, medium-hard midsole, resulting in a medium-firm shoe with slight downturn and slight asymmetry.
recommendations.tradeoffs: Tradeoff: Slightly wider in the forefoot, slightly tighter in the heel, and slightly stiffer than we target.
